summary:
The Vandal Kingdom of North Africa (429–534 AD) was established when the Vandals — having crossed from Spain into North Africa in 429 under King Gaiseric — conquered the prosperous Roman provinces of North Africa and established a state centered on Carthage that proved remarkably durable for over a century. The crossing itself was extraordinary: Gaiseric led approximately 80,000 people (warriors and families) across the Strait of Gibraltar on ships, an operation of logistical complexity that demonstrates sophisticated military planning. Within ten years, the Vandals controlled all of Roman North Africa except Carthage, which fell in 439 AD.

From Carthage, the Vandals built a powerful naval force that dominated the western Mediterranean, raiding Sicily, Sardinia, Corsica, the Balearic Islands, and the Italian coast. Gaiseric's navy made the [[event:2037|sack of Rome]] in 455 AD possible and briefly made the Vandal Kingdom a major naval power — the dominant force in the western Mediterranean basin. The Vandal kings adopted many Roman administrative and cultural practices while maintaining their Arian Christianity in tension with the Nicene Catholic majority they ruled. Despite some sophisticated cultural mixing, Vandal rule over the Roman population involved periodic religious persecution of Catholics. The kingdom was destroyed in 533–534 AD by the Byzantine general Belisarius with remarkable speed, ending Vandal power permanently — the Vandals were absorbed into the Byzantine and later Arab populations of North Africa.
